source_metadata.title_raw: Steven T. Mattas - A-18-193 - October 25, 2018 - Oakland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Steven T. Mattas 
Senior Principal 
Meyers Nave 
555 12th Street, Suite 1500 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-193 
Dear Mr. Mattas: 
October 25, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the Act). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does Councilmember Wu of the Town of Los Altos Hills (the Town) have a conflict of 
interest in decisions regarding three proposals to improve Town Hall facilities? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property interest in her 
residence. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
A. 
The Councilmember's Residence and the Town Hall Proposals. 
File No. A-18-193 
Page No. 2 
Councilmember Wu owns and resides in a single-family residence on a street behind the 
Town Hall location, which is estimated to be just slightly under 500 feet from her private property 
line. There are several other private residences in the immediate surrounding area that lie between 
the councilmember's property and Town Hall. 
You provide the following facts: 
The Los Altos Hills Town Hall, located at 26379 Fremont Road, Los Altos Hills, is a fully 
operational structure that consists of one main building that houses the Council Chambers 
and various Town departments, and a separate structure that houses the Parks and 
Recreations Services. Also, adjacent to the Town Hall building is the Heritage House, which 
functions as the Emergency Operations Center and a substation for the Santa Clara County 
Sheriff Office, as well as office space for staff. 
The Council and the majority of its commissions and committees meet in the Council 
Chambers. The facilities at Town Hall also .host various community and recreational 
programs for members of the public. For example, the Council Chambers served as the 
location for the Los Altos Hills Youth Commission to host its 7th annual Leadership 
Conference and technology workshops for seniors. Fitness programs such as yoga, Pilates, 
senior fitness, and boot camp sessions offered by private trainers and instructors also takes 
place weekly at the Council Chambers. Likewise, community arts programs such as 
exhibitions, displays and painting demonstrations also rent their activity space at Town Hall. 
Additionally, the Parks and Recreations building hosts cooking lessons, gardening 
workshops, adult education classes and youth recreational programs. 
In 2018, the City Council created a Facilities and Space Needs Subcommittee 
("Subcommittee") to evaluate the expansion of Town Hall facilities to accommodate 
additional meeting rooms, office space, a separate multipurpose room, and space for the 
Town's Emergency Operation Center. To date, the Subcommittee has identified three 
proposals that would either expand existing Town Hall buildings or construct new spaces. 
Proposal I would extend the south side of the main Town Hall structure to connect with the 
Parks and Recreation building. The existing Parks and Recreation building will be tom 
down and replaced with a new one. The existing vehicle turnaround in front of the Parks and 
Recs building will also be replaced with a new structure. This proposal is intended to 
provide additional space with minimal construction costs, service disruption and 
neighborhood impact. 
Proposal 2 would enclose the existing open patio space behind Council Chambers at the 
main Town Hall building and convert the area into usable indoor space. The additional 
space would accommodate the Town's Emergency Operations Center currently situated at 
the Heritage House. The conversion would not change the building footprint to the Town 
Hall main building but instead would enclose existing covered patio space. Proposal 3 
would create additional green space to the land currently owned by the Town and adjacent 


File No. A-18-193 
Page No. 3 
to the Town Hall building. With this option, the Town would not construct an indoor 
inhabitable area, but would create an open space seating and resting area. The area would be 
constructed with landscape planning and aesthetic design, to allow Town staff and members 
of the public an area for break time and leisure. 
B. The Town's Appraisal. 
The Town retained a licensed real estate appraiser to conduct a comparative market value 
study of the properties and the proposed Town Hall improvements.2 The appraiser reviewed the 
three proposed expansion plans and concluded that it is reasonably foreseeable that the Town's 
decision to utilize one or more of the proposed options would not result in any financial impact on 
the Councilmember's property, and thus the fair market value of her property would be unchanged. 
ANALYSIS 
A. Legal Standard. 
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) Interests that 
may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. Of 
those interests, the facts presented demonstrate that the Councilmember has the following interests 
in the decisions relating to the Town Hall proposals: 
• 
A real property interest in her residence, assuming she has a direct or indirect interest in her 
residence worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section 
87103.)3 
B. Foreseeability and Materiality. 
A decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an official's interest is explicitly involved if it is a 
named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870l(a).) When an official's interest is 
not explicitly involved, the decision's effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) The 
Councilmember's real property interest in her residence is not explicitly involved in the decisions 
relating to the Town Hall proposals. 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an 
official's real property interest. As set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1 ), if a decision involves 
2 You provided "excerpts from appraisal report" with your request. 
3 If a governmental decision affects a business entity or real property in which an official has an interest, 
materiality is determined by looking at the materiality standards for those interests instead. (Regulation l 8702.S(c).) 
Therefore, we do not separately analyze the Councilmember's interest in her personal finances. 


File No. A-18-193 
Page No. 4 
property within 500 feet of the official's real property interest, then the decision's effect on the 
official's interest is material unless there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. 
Based on the facts provided, the approval of any one proposal would be a rejection of the 
other two proposals. Consequently, if a decision's effect on the Councilmember's real property is 
material when applied to any one of the Town Hall proposals, then it is material to all three 
proposals. 
Proposal 1 involves new construction and other improvements that would provide more 
space for the Town and for the community and members of the public who use Town Hall facilities. 
The south side of the main buildings would expand to connect with a new Parks and Recreation 
building, the existing Parks and Recreation building would be tom down and replaced with a new 
one, and a new structure would be built on an existing vehicle turnaround. Proposal 3 would create 
an open space and resting area, with improvements to the landscape and enhanced aesthetics. 
Proposals 1 and 3 involve significant improvements and enhancements that would occur just 
under 500 feet from the Councilmember's residential real property. Thus, we cannot conclude the 
decisions relating to the Town Hall proposals would not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable 
impact on the Councilmember's real property interest in her residence. The Act prohibits the 
Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on her real property interest in her residence. 
C. The Excerpt of Appraisal. 
Reasonable reliance on an appraisal by a disinterested and otherwise qualified real estate 
professional, based on an accurate understanding of all pertinent facts and circumstances and proper 
application of pertinent Commission regulations, will generally be considered a good-faith effort by 
a public official to assess the financial effect of a decision on his or her real property. However, an 
appraisal concluding that there will be no financial effect on an official's real property only 
provides evidence that the presumption has been rebutted. An appraisal providing this conclusion, 
must provide relevant and sufficient support for its findings. 
In this case, the appraisal does not properly apply the Commission's regulations. The 
appraisal finds that it is reasonably foreseeable that the Town's decision to utilize one or more of 
the proposed options would not result in any financial impact on the Councilmember's property. 
However, the appraisal fails to address whether it is also reasonably foreseeable that there may be a 
financial impact on the property. These finding are not mutually exclusive. Reasonably foreseeable 
does not mean more probably than not. In fact, under Regulation 18701(b), a financial effect need 
not even be likely to be reasonably foreseeable. In other words, the appraisal provided erroneously 
addresses the foreseeability that a decision may not have a financial effect as opposed to the 
foreseeability that the decision will have a financial impact. 
Accordingly, in the present case, the excerpt of appraisal fails to provide sufficient facts to 
rebut the presumption of materiality under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1). 


File No. A-18-193 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JMF,Jr.:JGL 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Cou 
' 
,le~ 
Feser Jr. 
s-enior Commission Counsel 
egal Division
